1: wĕyôsēp hûraḏ miṣrāymāh wayyiqnēhû potîpar sĕrîs parʿōh sar haṭṭabbāḥîm ʾîš miṣrî miyyaḏ hayišmĕʿēlîm ʾăšer hôrĕḏuhû šāmmāh — "Joseph had been brought down to Egypt. Potiphar, a court official of Pharaoh, captain of the guard (sar haṭṭabbāḥîm), an Egyptian, bought him from the Ishmaelites." Hûraḏ (Hophal of yāraḏ) — "was brought down" — the passive descent; Joseph is not acting but being acted upon. Sar haṭṭabbāḥîm (captain of the slaughterers/bodyguard, H2876 ṭabbāḥ) — ṭabbāḥ literally means slaughterer/butcher; the title refers to the royal executioner-bodyguard, the officer responsible for the king's personal security and capital punishment. The same title appears in 2 Kgs 25:8 for Nebuchadnezzar's commander who destroys Jerusalem. Joseph has been bought by the man who controls life and death in Pharaoh's household — a narratively significant placement.
2: wayhî YHWH ʾet-yôsēp wayhî ʾîš maṣlîaḥ wayhî bĕḇêt ʾăḏōnāyw hammiṣrî — "YHWH was with Joseph, so he prospered (maṣlîaḥ); and he lived in the house of his Egyptian master." The formula YHWH ʾet-yôsēp (YHWH was with Joseph) — the covenant-presence formula of the patriarchal tradition: "I will be with you" (26:3 to Isaac; 28:15 to Jacob; 31:3 to Jacob). This is the first time in the Joseph narrative that YHWH is named as an active agent; the name had been absent since Jacob's last theophany (35:9-15). Maṣlîaḥ (prospering, Hiphil participle of ṣālaḥ, H6743, to succeed, advance, thrive) — the prosperity is explicitly attributed to divine presence, not to Joseph's native ability. The pattern will repeat at v21 and 23, forming the structural skeleton of the chapter.
3: wayyarʾ ʾăḏōnāyw kî YHWH ʾittô wĕḵōl ʾăšer-hûʾ ʿōśeh YHWH maṣlîaḥ bĕyāḏô — "His master saw that YHWH was with him and that YHWH gave him success in everything he did." The remarkable statement that an Egyptian recognized YHWH's presence in Joseph — a pagan slave-master discerning the divine hand behind his slave's work. This recognition pattern — non-Israelites seeing YHWH's blessing on the covenant people — recurs throughout the Joseph narrative (39:5; 40:15 implies Potiphar's household; 41:38-39 Pharaoh) and foreshadows the Exodus account where Pharaoh and Egypt will be forced to recognize YHWH (Exod 9:16; 14:18). Maṣlîaḥ bĕyāḏô (succeeded in his hand) — "in his hand" frames success as something YHWH places in Joseph's hands.
4: wayyimṣāʾ yôsēp ḥēn bĕʿênāyw wayyĕšāret ʾōtô wayyapqidēhû ʿal-bêtô wĕḵol-yeš-lô nātan bĕyāḏô — "Joseph found favor (ḥēn) in his eyes and became his attendant. He put him in charge of his household and entrusted to his care all that he owned." Māṣāʾ ḥēn (found grace/favor) — the covenant-favor formula (6:8 for Noah; 30:27 for Laban; 32:5 for Jacob before Esau). Mĕšārēt (personal attendant/minister, Piel participle of šārat, H8334, to serve, minister) — the term used for Joshua as Moses's attendant (Exod 24:13; Josh 1:1), for temple service, and for angelic service (Ps 103:21). Joseph's role in Potiphar's household mirrors the role of a Joseph-type in every subsequent context: elevated to administrator, entrusted with total care.
5: wayhî mēʾāz hipqîḏ ʾōtô bĕḇêtô wĕʿal kol-ʾăšer yĕš-lô wayyĕḇāreḵ YHWH ʾet-bêt hammiṣrî biglal yôsēp — "From the time he put him in charge of his household and all he owned, YHWH blessed the Egyptian's household for Joseph's sake." The blessing-formula wayyĕḇāreḵ YHWH ʾet-bêt hammiṣrî biglal yôsēp is the Abrahamic covenant promise extending outward through Joseph: "I will bless those who bless you" (12:3). The Egyptian household is blessed not because of anything Egyptian, but through the covenant-bearer in their midst. The same pattern will appear in 39:23 (prison), 41:37-45 (all of Egypt), and later in Daniel, Esther, and Ezra — the covenant people in exile blessing the surrounding nations.
6: wĕyôsēp hāyāh yĕpeh-tōʾar wîpeh marʾeh — "Now Joseph was well-built and handsome (yĕpeh-tōʾar wîpeh marʾeh)." The double beauty formula — yĕpeh-tōʾar (beautiful of form/shape) + yĕpeh marʾeh (beautiful of appearance/face) — is identical to the description of his mother Rachel in 29:17 (yĕpat-tōʾar wîpat marʾeh). Joseph inherits Rachel's beauty formula — a narrative signal connecting mother and son that the reader of the prior chapters will recognize. The same superlative double appears for Esther (Est 2:7) and David is described with a similar formula (1 Sam 16:12). The beauty serves the narrative: Potiphar's wife will see and desire (v7), echoing the pattern of the predatory gaze (Shechem/Dinah in 34:2).
7: wayhî ʾaḥar haddĕḇārîm hāʾēlleh waṯṯiśśāʾ ʾēšet-ʾăḏōnāyw ʾet-ʿênêhā ʾel-yôsēp waṯṯōʾmer šiḵḇāh ʿimmî — "After these things his master's wife cast her eyes on Joseph and said, 'Lie with me!'" Waṯṯiśśāʾ... ʾet-ʿênêhā (she lifted her eyes) — the predatory gaze construction used for Shechem (34:2: "he saw her"), for Lot's wife (19:26), and for Laban (29:1: Jacob lifted his feet after looking). Here it is the woman who is the desiring agent. The invitation šiḵḇāh ʿimmî (lie with me) is bare, direct, and without the rhetorical setup Potiphar's wife will provide in v17 when she accuses Joseph — there the same words become a false accusation.
8: wayĕmāʾēn wayyōʾmer ʾel-ʾēšet ʾăḏōnāyw hēn ʾăḏōnî lōʾ-yāḏaʿ ʾittî mah-babbāyit wĕḵōl ʾăšer-yeš-lô nātan bĕyāḏî — "But he refused. He said to his master's wife, 'My master does not concern himself with anything in the house; everything he owns he has put in my care.'" Joseph's refusal is grounded in the covenant-faithfulness to Potiphar: the trust placed in him. Wayĕmāʾēn (he refused, Piel of māʾen, H3985) — a strong refusal-verb; used for Moses's refusal to be called Pharaoh's son (Heb 11:24), for Israel's refusal to be comforted (Gen 37:35), and for Pharaoh's refusal to let Israel go (Exod 7:14). Joseph's refusal of Potiphar's wife anticipates these later refusals-in-faith.
9: ʾêḵāh ʾeʿĕśeh hārāʿāh hag-gĕḏōlāh hazzōʾt wĕḥāṭāʾtî lēʾlōhîm — "How could I do this great evil and sin against God?" Wĕḥāṭāʾtî lēʾlōhîm (and sin against God, H430) — Joseph grounds the refusal not only in loyalty to Potiphar (rāʿāh lēʾḏōnî, evil against my master, v8) but in theological ethics: sin against God. The word ḥāṭāʾ (H2398, to sin, to miss the mark, to commit a moral offense) — the standard OT term for moral and religious transgression. Joseph's theological framing of sexual ethics is remarkable in the narrative context: he identifies the sin as directed against God, not merely as a breach of social loyalty. This anticipates the covenant ethic of the Decalogue (Exod 20:14) and psalmic wisdom (Ps 51:4: "against you, you only, have I sinned").
10: wayhî kĕḏabbĕrāh ʾel-yôsēp yôm yôm wĕlōʾ-šāmaʿ ʾēleyhā liškōḇ ʾeṣlāh lihyôt ʿimmāh — "She spoke to Joseph day after day; he refused to lie beside her or be with her." The phrase yôm yôm (day after day, day by day) — the persistence of the temptation. The narrative's insistence on Joseph's sustained refusal over repeated solicitation is theologically significant: his virtue is not a single act of will but a sustained pattern. Wĕlōʾ-šāmaʿ ʾēleyhā (he did not listen to her) — using the hearing-verb šāmaʿ for moral compliance: to "hear" someone was to obey; his refusal to "hear" her is his moral consistency.
11: wayhî kĕhayyôm hazzeh wayyāḇōʾ habbāytāh laʿăśôt mĕlaʾḵtô wĕʾên ʾîš mēʾanĕšê habbayit šām babbāyit — "One day he went into the house to attend to his duties, and none of the household servants was inside." The narrative detail — no household servants present — is the circumstantial condition that makes Potiphar's wife's entrapment possible. Laʿăśôt mĕlaʾḵtô (to do his work/task) — Joseph is present for professional duty, not for personal desire. The absence of witnesses is the condition for both the assault and the false accusation that follows; there is no corroborating testimony for either party.
12: waṯṯiṯpĕśēhû bĕḇiḡḏô lēʾmōr šiḵḇāh ʿimmî wayyaʿăzōḇ biḡḏô bĕyāḏāh wayyānos wayyēṣēʾ haḥûṣāh — "She grabbed him by his garment (ḇegeḏ) saying 'Lie with me!' But he left his garment in her hand and fled outside." Begeḏ (H899, garment/cloak) — garments in the Joseph narrative are consistently the material sign of identity, status, and evidence: the coat of many colors (37:3,23,31-32: torn and blood-stained as evidence of death), this garment (39:12-16: as evidence of assault), the royal robes of ch 41 (as evidence of elevation), the five changes of clothes for Benjamin (45:22). Joseph leaves his begeḏ as involuntary evidence — the same mechanism by which his coat was used against him in ch 37. Wayyānos (he fled, from nûs, H5127) — the same verb used in Exod 14:25 for the Egyptians fleeing from Israel and in Joseph's blessing (49:21).
13: wayhî kirʾōtāh kî-ʿāzaḇ biḡḏô bĕyāḏāh wayyānos haḥûṣāh — "When she saw that he had left his garment in her hand and fled outside…" The begeḏ becomes the evidence-item for her false accusation. The same garment that Potiphar's wife grabbed as an instrument of seduction is now transformed into an instrument of accusation. The repeated mention of the garment (vv12,13,15,16,18) shows that the narrator wants the reader to track the garment's transformation from seduction-tool to false-evidence.
14: waṯṯiqrāʾ lĕʾanĕšê ḇêtāh waṯṯōʾmer lāhem lēʾmōr rĕʾû hēḇîʾ lānû ʾîš ʿiḇrî lĕṣaḥeq ḇānû — "She called to the men of the household and said, 'See, he brought us a Hebrew (ʿiḇrî) to mock us!'" ʿIḇrî (Hebrew, H5680, from ʿēḇer, H5677 — one from beyond/the other side; the ethnic designation for Israelites used by outsiders) — Potiphar's wife uses the ethnic label as a derogatory marker: "this Hebrew" (ʾîš ʿiḇrî) dehumanizes Joseph by his foreignness. In the Joseph narrative, "Hebrew" is always used by Egyptians about Joseph (39:14,17; 41:12) or by Joseph self-identifying to the cupbearer (40:15). The ethnic insult functions to deflect suspicion from herself by making Joseph's alleged aggression a racial as well as personal offense.
15: wayhî kĕhārîmî qôlî wāʾeqrāʾ wayyaʿăzōḇ beḡḏô ʾeṣlî wayyānos haḥûṣāh — "When I raised my voice and screamed, he left his garment beside me and ran outside." The false accusation inverts the narrative: her grabbing becomes his fleeing; his leaving the garment (which she grabbed) becomes evidence of his assault. The begeḏ left "beside me" (ʾeṣlî) is the linchpin of her case — physical evidence she controls and can present to Potiphar. The identical language between her accusation and the narrator's account (vv12-13) allows the reader to track what actually happened versus what she claims.
16: waṯṯannah beḡḏô ʾeṣlāh ʿaḏ-bôʾ ʾăḏōnāyw ʾel-bêtô — "She kept his garment beside her until his master came home." The preserved garment is deliberate: it functions as exhibit A. The time gap — keeping it until Potiphar returns — suggests she is preparing her narrative rather than acting in immediate outrage. The narrator notes this without comment; the detail itself indicts her.
17: waṯṯĕḏabbēr ʾēlāyw kaddĕḇārîm hāʾēlleh lēʾmōr bāʾ-ʾēlay hāʿeḇeḏ hāʿiḇrî ʾăšer-hēḇēʾtā lānû lĕṣaḥeq bî — "She told him this same story: 'The Hebrew slave (hāʿeḇeḏ hāʿiḇrî) you brought us came to me to mock me.'" Now the double label is applied: hāʿeḇeḏ hāʿiḇrî (the Hebrew slave) — both his slave-status and his ethnicity are deployed as the accusation's framing. Before the servants (v14) she said "a Hebrew man"; before Potiphar she says "the Hebrew slave" — the second formulation adds the social-status insult to the ethnic one. The phrase implicates Potiphar himself ("you brought us") — she holds him partially responsible for bringing this danger into their house.
18: wayhî kĕhārîmî qôlî wāʾeqrāʾ wayyaʿăzōḇ beḡḏô ʾeṣlî wayyānos haḥûṣāh — "When I raised my voice and screamed, he left his garment beside me and ran outside." The verbatim repetition of v15 is the narrator's device for emphasizing the lie's consistency — she tells the same story twice to make it stick. The repetition also underscores what the reader already knows to be false, creating the tension between testimony and truth that will only be resolved by God's providence (41:1).
19: wayhî kišmōaʿ ʾăḏōnāyw ʾet-diḇrê ʾištô ʾăšer dibbĕrāh ʾēlāyw lēʾmōr kaddĕḇārîm hāʾēlleh ʿāśāh lî ʿaḇdeḵā wayyiḥar ʾappô — "When his master heard his wife's story, his anger burned." Wayyiḥar ʾappô (his anger burned, H2734 + H639 — literally "his nose burned hot") — the standard idiom for intense anger; ʾap (nose) because the nostrils flare with rage. The narrator does not say Potiphar believed his wife; only that his anger burned. The subsequent punishment (imprisonment rather than execution — the expected penalty for rape of a master's wife) may suggest Potiphar's partial skepticism: he punishes but does not execute, possibly because he knows Joseph's character.
20: wayyiqqaḥ ʾăḏōnê yôsēp ʾōtô wayyittĕnēhû ʾel-bêt hassohar mĕqôm ʾăšer-ʾăsûrê hammelek ʾăsûrîm — "Joseph's master put him in the prison (bêt hassohar), the place where the king's prisoners were held." Bêt hassohar (house of the sohar, H5470) — sohar appears to be an Egyptian loanword or a Hebrew word for a circular/round place (sāḥar, H5437, to go around); possibly referring to an Egyptian-style round or pit-prison. The location — "where the king's prisoners were confined" — is a royal facility, higher-status than a common jail. This placement is significant: Joseph will meet Pharaoh's own officials (the cupbearer and baker in ch 40) precisely because they are imprisoned in the same royal facility. What appears to be a punishment is actually a providential placement.
21: wayhî YHWH ʾet-yôsēp wayyēṭ ʾēlāyw ḥāseḏ wayyittēn ḥinnô bĕʿênê śar bêt-hassohar — "But YHWH was with Joseph and showed him ḥeseḏ (covenant-loyalty/lovingkindness) and gave him favor (ḥēn) in the eyes of the prison warden." The renewal of the YHWH ʾet-yôsēp formula (from v2) at the transition to prison is the structural signal: the divine presence does not depend on circumstance. Ḥeseḏ (H2617, covenant-loyalty, steadfast love, lovingkindness) — the most theologically dense word in the OT's relational vocabulary; it carries the weight of covenant obligation, faithfulness to relationship, and active benevolence. YHWH's ḥeseḏ is the foundation of the psalmic covenant vocabulary (Ps 118:1; 136:1-26; Lam 3:22-23). Joseph in prison receives the same covenant-love that the patriarchs received in freedom.
22: wayyittēn śar bêt-hassohar bĕyaḏ-yôsēp ʾēt kol-hāʾăsûrîm ʾăšer bĕḇêt-hassohar wĕʾēt kol-ʾăšer ʿōśîm šām hûʾ hāyāh ʿōśeh — "The warden put Joseph in charge of all the prisoners and everything done there." The prison chapter exactly mirrors the Potiphar chapter: in vv2-6 Potiphar saw YHWH was with Joseph, entrusted everything to his care, and was unconcerned about anything; in vv21-23 the warden sees YHWH is with Joseph, entrusts everything to his care, and is unconcerned about anything. The mirror structure establishes a pattern: wherever Joseph is placed, YHWH turns it into a place of authority-delegation. The structural repetition is the theological argument.
23: ʾên śar bêt-hassohar rōʾeh ʾet-kol-mĕʾûmāh bĕyāḏô baʾăšer YHWH ʾittô waʾăšer-hûʾ ʿōśeh YHWH maṣlîaḥ — "The warden paid no attention to anything in Joseph's care, because YHWH was with him; and whatever he did YHWH made it prosper (maṣlîaḥ)." The closing formula mirrors the opening of the chapter (v2: "YHWH was with Joseph and he was a maṣlîaḥ man") — maṣlîaḥ (Hiphil of ṣālaḥ) bookends the chapter. The chapter structure is: YHWH is with Joseph (v2) → Potiphar entrusts everything (vv3-6) → temptation and accusation (vv7-19) → prison (v20) → YHWH is with Joseph (v21) → warden entrusts everything (vv22-23). The covenant presence is the constant; the circumstances are the variable.
